description: Diagnostic dashboard for triaging vLLM performance, structured around the 5-step workflow from the Red Hat Developer article (March 2026). Each row corresponds to one diagnostic step: latency decomposition, server saturation, KV cache / VRAM health, sequence-length context, and distributed inference signals.

links.title: Source article
links.tooltip: 5 steps to triage vLLM performance (Red Hat Developer)

panels.title: Step 1 — Isolate the symptom: TTFT vs. ITL

panels.description:
Fleet-level headline panel: three series, aggregated across all selected pods.

• p95 latency (solid, left axis): histogram_quantile computed over bucket-sums across pods — the correct method, since taking the max or mean of per-pod p95s misrepresents the true tail.
• avg latency (dashed, left axis): sum(rate(latency_sum)) / sum(rate(latency_count)) — request-weighted across pods, not a naive mean of pod averages.
• throughput (filled area, right axis): total successful req/s across pods.

This panel deliberately fixes p95 (not the dashboard's percentile selector) so config A/B comparisons always show the same SLO-relevant tail. The selector still controls TTFT, ITL, ISL/OSL, and Step 5 panels.
panels.title: E2E request latency (p95 + avg) and throughput — fleet aggregate

panels.description:
TTFT decomposed at the fleet level: queueing delay + prefill compute.

• p95 TTFT (solid, left axis): tail latency for the first token, the SLO-relevant figure.
• avg TTFT (dashed, left axis): request-weighted mean.
• prefill throughput (filled area, right axis): total prompt-tokens/sec aggregated across pods, computed from vllm:prompt_tokens_total. This is the natural companion to TTFT because prefill ends at first token.

Reading guide: if TTFT rises while prefill throughput also rises, you're prefill compute-saturated (more long prompts arriving). If TTFT rises while prefill throughput is flat or falling, the cause is queueing — confirm with Step 2's running/waiting panel.
panels.title: Time to First Token (p95 + avg) and prefill token throughput

panels.description:
ITL at the fleet level — the per-token cost during decode.

• p95 ITL (solid, left axis): tail of the inter-token interval. High p95 with low median = batching spikes from long-prompt arrivals.
• avg ITL (dashed, left axis): request-weighted mean.
• decode throughput (filled area, right axis): total generation-tokens/sec across pods, computed from vllm:generation_tokens_total. This is the 'tokens/sec the user sees' rate.

Reading guide: throughput up + ITL up = bigger batches (the throughput/latency trade-off in action). ITL up while throughput flat = bandwidth or interconnect issue, not batching.
panels.title: Inter-Token Latency (p95 + avg) and decode token throughput

panels.description: Running = requests actively on GPU (batch size). Waiting = queue depth. If Waiting is consistently > 0, the server is saturated and TTFT will rise due to queueing. If Waiting is 0 but TTFT is still high, the bottleneck is prefill compute (long prompts), not queueing.
panels.title: Running vs. waiting requests
Step 3 — Evaluate VRAM and KV cache health

panels.description: Fleet KV cache occupancy — average and max across pods. The max line is the leading indicator: if one pod hits 95% while the fleet average is 70%, you have a hot pod that will start preempting before the average tells you anything is wrong. Sustained max ≥ 90% means memory pressure somewhere. Remediation per the article: quantize weights (FP8/AWQ/GPTQ), quantize KV cache (--kv-cache-dtype fp8), reduce --max-num-seqs, scale out, or use a smaller model.
panels.title: KV cache occupancy

panels.description: Preemption rate — requests stopped mid-generation to free memory. Any sustained rate above zero indicates memory thrashing and causes severe latency spikes. Remediation: lower --max-num-seqs so the scheduler doesn't admit more than the KV cache can sustain.
panels.title: Preemption rate (req/s) — should be ~0

panels.description: Prefix cache hit rate — share of token lookups that reused cached prompt prefixes. High values (e.g. multi-turn chat, RAG with stable system prompts) directly reduce TTFT and compute. Low values in a RAG/chat workload may indicate the prompt isn't structured prefix-first.
panels.title: Prefix cache hit rate

panels.description:
ISL distribution across the fleet.

• p95 (solid): tail of the prompt length distribution.
• avg (dashed): mean prompt length.

Histogram aggregation uses sum-then-quantile (the only correct method) for p95, and sum(rate(_sum)) / sum(rate(_count)) for avg.

Why this matters: long prompts drive prefill cost and TTFT.
panels.title: Input sequence length — ISL tokens (p95 + avg)

panels.description:
OSL distribution across the fleet.

• p95 (solid): tail of the output length distribution.
• avg (dashed): mean output length.

Histogram aggregation uses sum-then-quantile (the only correct method) for p95, and sum(rate(_sum)) / sum(rate(_count)) for avg.

Why this matters: long outputs drive total latency linearly via ITL.
panels.title: Output sequence length — OSL tokens (p95 + avg)

panels.description:
Fleet-aggregate token throughput, split into prefill (prompt) and decode (generation) tokens/sec.

The visible gap is informative: prefill is parallel (per scheduler step it can process thousands of tokens at once), decode is sequential (one token per request per step). Roughly the same number of req/s through the system produces a much higher prefill rate than decode rate — this is the asymmetry that makes ISL drive TTFT and OSL drive total latency.

A sudden drop in decode rate with prefill rate steady usually means decode is being preempted (check Step 3).
panels.title: Token throughput — prefill vs decode (fleet aggregate)

panels.description: Fleet-aggregate snapshot of the four key signals: tail latency, tail ITL, total throughput, average KV occupancy. With per-pod divergence detection consolidated away, this is the at-a-glance health summary. If you need to inspect individual pods, switch the $pod selector to a single pod.
panels.title: Fleet summary — p95 / req/s / KV occupancy

panels.description: Fleet-level ITL with min/max envelope. The min and max lines bracket the spread across pods without drawing one line per pod. When max diverges sharply from p95, you have an outlier pod — typically an interconnect issue (PCIe vs NVLink, cross-NUMA). Confirm with `nvidia-smi topo -m` on the affected node.
panels.title: ITL — fleet p95 / min / max envelope

panels.description: Legacy NVML-style GPU utilization — fraction of time at least one CUDA kernel was executing on the GPU. This is what `nvidia-smi`, the Kubernetes device plugin, and most autoscalers see. Keep it visible for cross-referencing with external tooling, but DO NOT rely on it alone for LLM inference tuning: a value near 100% only means 'some kernel is running', not 'compute pipes are saturated'. The profiling row below (SM Active / Occupancy / Tensor / DRAM) tells you what kind of work is actually happening. Classic LLM-decode pattern: GPU_UTIL ≈ 100% while Tensor Active stays low and DRAM Active is high — the card is busy moving weights and KV, not doing matmuls.
panels.title: GPU_UTIL (NVML legacy — for cross-reference with nvidia-smi)

panels.description: SM activity — fraction of cycles each Streaming Multiprocessor had at least one warp assigned. Higher is better for batch/throughput workloads. For LLM inference, sustained SM_ACTIVE well below 1.0 usually means either small batch (memory-bound decode) or scheduler under-utilization — confirm by cross-referencing with running batch size in Step 2. NOTE: DCGM_FI_PROF_* metrics are reported as ratios (0.0–1.0) in recent dcgm-exporter; older versions emit percent (0–100). Adjust the unit if needed.
panels.title: SM Active

panels.description: SM Occupancy — fraction of warp slots filled on each SM, averaged over the sampling period. Where SM_ACTIVE asks 'is the SM doing anything?', occupancy asks 'how saturated is its scheduling capacity?'. Low occupancy with high SM_ACTIVE is the classic LLM-decode signature: kernels are launching but each has too few warps to hide memory latency — the GPU is starved by skinny GEMMs reading the KV cache. Remediation is the same as for DRAM-bound decode: larger effective batch (raise --max-num-seqs if KV headroom allows), or shrink per-step bytes via FP8 / KV quantization. Note: PROF_SM_OCCUPANCY is sampled, not a true cycle-accurate count.
panels.title: SM Occupancy

panels.description: Tensor pipe activity — fraction of cycles the tensor (HMMA) pipe is doing matrix-multiply work. This is your most direct signal of useful AI compute. Low values during high request load usually mean decode is memory-bandwidth-bound (skinny GEMMs in autoregressive generation), not that the GPU is idle. Multiply by the GPU's marketed peak TFLOPS to estimate effective TFLOPS (e.g. H100 FP8: × 1979).
panels.title: Tensor Core Active (HMMA pipe)

panels.description: DRAM activity — fraction of cycles the GPU memory interface is reading or writing. This is the single most relevant utilization signal for vLLM decode: autoregressive generation re-reads model weights and KV cache for every token, so high DRAM_ACTIVE with modest TENSOR_ACTIVE is the classic memory-bandwidth-bound signature. Remediation per the article: FP8 quantization shrinks the per-step bytes read, moving you off this bound.
panels.title: DRAM Active (memory bandwidth) — the decode bottleneck signal

panels.description: Framebuffer (VRAM) used vs. total per GPU. Total is computed as USED + FREE since dcgm-exporter's default CSV exposes both but not a TOTAL field directly. vLLM pre-allocates KV cache space at startup based on --gpu-memory-utilization (default 0.9), so USED usually sits flat near that fraction of TOTAL; sharp drops indicate the engine released the KV pool, sharp rises with a fresh deployment mean the model load completed. The visual gap between USED and TOTAL is your headroom — small headroom is expected and intentional in vLLM, but if you push --gpu-memory-utilization too aggressively this gap closes and the pod becomes vulnerable to OOM under load.
panels.title: GPU framebuffer memory (used / total)

panels.description:
GPU power draw vs. the configured power management limit per GPU.

• Solid line: current power draw (DCGM_FI_DEV_POWER_USAGE).
• Dashed line: power limit (DCGM_FI_DEV_POWER_MGMT_LIMIT) — the cap above which the GPU will start clocking down to stay within budget.

Healthy inference draw typically sits at 60–85% of the limit under load. Sustained draw at or near the dashed line, especially combined with nonzero values in the power-violation panel to the right, means the card is being throttled and you're leaving performance on the table. Possible remedies: increase the power limit (`nvidia-smi -pl <watts>` on the node, if admin policy allows) or reduce per-GPU concurrency (lower --max-num-seqs, scale out replicas).
panels.title: GPU power draw vs. power limit

panels.description: GPU and memory temperature. Throttling typically kicks in around 83–88°C depending on SKU. Sustained high temperatures correlate with the throttling-violation counters on the right.
panels.title: GPU & memory temperature

panels.description: Throttling violation duration rate (ns/s). Any sustained nonzero rate on POWER or THERMAL means the GPU is clamping clocks, which directly hurts ITL. Note: these counters are commented out in default-counters.csv on dcgm-exporter — they must be explicitly enabled in your metrics ConfigMap. If the panel is empty, that's the most likely reason.
panels.title: Throttling violations (power / thermal)

panels.description: PCIe replay counter rate — retries on the PCIe link. A nonzero rate indicates link-level errors that can stall host↔GPU and (on systems without NVLink) GPU↔GPU traffic — directly relevant to the interconnect overhead discussion in Step 5. Pair with `nvidia-smi topo -m` to see whether traffic is going over PCIe vs NVLink.
panels.title: PCIe replay rate (interconnect health)

panels.description: FP16 non-tensor pipe activity — fraction of cycles the FP16 CUDA cores (NOT the tensor cores) are doing work. Captures FP16 elementwise / non-matmul work: activations, softmax, layernorm, RoPE, and any custom kernels routed through FP16 cores. For standard vLLM inference this typically sits at low values because tensor cores dominate; spikes here usually mean a custom sampler, beam search, non-fused attention variant, or non-tensor-core-friendly kernel path is active. NOT a substitute for PIPE_TENSOR_ACTIVE (matmul lives there).
panels.title: FP16 pipe active (non-tensor)

panels.description: FP32 non-tensor pipe activity — fraction of cycles the FP32 CUDA cores are doing work. In an FP16/BF16 inference workload this should stay near zero; unexpectedly high values suggest a code path has dropped to FP32, often unintentionally. Common culprits: reduction accumulators in unfused kernels, FP32 master-weight copies in mixed-precision paths, logit processors operating in FP32, or a model loaded with the wrong dtype. Useful precision sanity check, not a primary triage metric.
panels.title: FP32 pipe active (non-tensor) — precision sanity check

panels.description:
Decision flow from the Red Hat Developer article. Use this as the operator runbook overlay for the panels above.

1. **Define success first** — throughput-sensitive, latency-sensitive, or bursty? Optimization differs.

2. **Symptom: TTFT vs ITL.** High E2E + high TTFT + low ITL → likely queueing or long prefill. High ITL → bandwidth-/batch-bound.

3. **Saturation.** num_requests_waiting > 0 sustained → admission-limited. waiting = 0 + high TTFT → prefill compute-bound.

4. **KV cache health.** Occupancy ≥ 90% or any preemption rate → memory pressure. Remediate with quantization (FP8/AWQ/GPTQ, --kv-cache-dtype fp8), --max-num-seqs reduction, smaller model, or more/larger GPUs.

5. **Sequence shape.** Long ISL drives TTFT; long OSL drives total latency via ITL. RAG: put static prefix first to maximize prefix cache hit.

6. **Distribution.** Use minimum TP that fits the model, then scale with replicas. 2× TP=2 usually beats 1× TP=4. Check `nvidia-smi topo -m` for PCIe-vs-NVLink penalties.

**GPU utilization (DCGM).** Tensor pipe high + DRAM moderate = compute-bound (prefill-dominated). Tensor pipe modest + DRAM high = memory-bandwidth-bound (decode-dominated, classic LLM inference). Any throttling-violation rate or PCIe replay rate above zero is a hardware-level issue masking software tuning.

**Remediation toolbox**: right-size model · scale hardware (more replicas; higher-bandwidth GPUs) · quantize weights + KV cache · speculative decoding (MTP, EAGLE3, draft, n-gram) · refine TP/DP mix.

Source: https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2026/03/09/5-steps-triage-vllm-performance

options.content:
## Triage runbook

| Step | What to look at | Likely cause if abnormal |
|------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| 1. Symptom | E2E, TTFT, ITL panels | High TTFT + low ITL → queueing or long prefill. High ITL → bandwidth/batch. |
| 2. Saturation | Running vs. waiting, queue depth | Waiting > 0 → admission-limited. Waiting = 0 but TTFT high → prefill compute-bound. |
| 3. KV cache | Occupancy, preemptions, prefix hit rate | ≥ 90% occupancy or any preemption → quantize / reduce `--max-num-seqs` / scale GPUs. |
| 4. Sequence shape | ISL & OSL panels | Long ISL → TTFT pressure. Long OSL → linear E2E pressure via ITL. RAG: prefix-first prompts. |
| 5. Distribution | Per-pod summary, p95 ITL per pod | Divergence between pods → check `nvidia-smi topo -m`; prefer replicas over high TP. |
| GPU (DCGM) | SM / Tensor / DRAM active, power, temp, throttling, PCIe replay | Tensor high + DRAM modest → compute-bound (prefill). Tensor modest + DRAM high → memory-bandwidth-bound (decode — classic LLM inference). Any throttling or PCIe replay > 0 → hardware-level cause that masks tuning. |

**Remediation toolbox** — right-size the model · add replicas or higher-bandwidth GPUs · quantize weights + KV cache (`--kv-cache-dtype fp8`) · speculative decoding (MTP, EAGLE3, draft model, n-gram) · refine TP / DP mix (min TP that fits, then scale out).

**Source** — [5 steps to triage vLLM performance, Red Hat Developer, Mar 2026](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2026/03/09/5-steps-triage-vllm-performance)

list.description: Latency percentile applied to all histogram_quantile() panels. Single value, so each chart shows one line per pod, making config comparisons across time / deployments visually clean.
list.label: Percentile
list.name: percentile

title: vLLM Performance Triage (5-step workflow)
